academicDisclaimer.title: 📘 A note about these numbers
academicDisclaimer.body: I set every default on this page from common sense and casual observation of small cafés, not by measuring any real store. Expanding "Design rationale" in each section shows how I estimated the number, which distribution it uses, and the literature that backs the distribution choice. The sidebar card "A note about these numbers" lays out the full methodology stance.

rationaleSection.noReferences: The numerical value here is a scenario assumption without literature support. Only the modeling framework (SimPy agent-based) can be referenced: Borshchev & Filippov (2004).

T.label: Random time variable
T.desc: T stands for 'how long this event takes' — each draw gives a different number. Capital T is the queueing-theory convention, stressing that it's a random variable and not a fixed value.
T.example: T ~ N(3, 0.6²) means one order might take 2.8 min, the next 3.3 min, clustering around 3.

X.label: Discrete outcome
X.desc: X is a single yes/no draw, either 0 or 1. Used for binary decisions like 'does the cat go rest now.'
X.example: X = 1 → goes to rest; X = 0 → keeps finding the next customer.

Exp.label: Exponential distribution Exp(λ)
Exp.desc: The distribution of spacing between random events. Its defining feature is memorylessness: no matter how long you've waited, the probability of the next event stays the same.
Exp.example: T ~ Exp(1/8) means one customer every 8 minutes on average, but actual gaps could be 30 s or 25 min.

Normal.label: Normal distribution N(μ, σ²)
Normal.desc: The classic bell curve. Samples cluster around mean μ and thin out further away. Service times use it because they have a natural floor and rarely wander long.
Normal.example: T ~ N(8, 1.6²) means most prep times land between 6 and 10 min; very few exceed 11.

Bernoulli.label: Bernoulli distribution
Bernoulli.desc: A single probabilistic draw — like flipping one biased coin. p is the chance of 1, and 1-p is the chance of 0.
Bernoulli.example: Bernoulli(0.2) → 20% chance to rest, 80% chance to find the next customer.

lambda.label: Rate parameter λ
lambda.desc: The intensity of the Exponential: how many events happen per unit time on average. Higher λ = more frequent events, with mean spacing = 1/λ.
lambda.example: λ = 1/8 means 0.125 events per minute on average, equivalent to one every 8 minutes.

mu.label: Mean μ
mu.desc: The center of a Normal distribution — where most samples cluster, and also the value the sample average converges to.
mu.example: μ = 3 means this activity takes 3 min on average.

sigma.label: Standard deviation σ
sigma.desc: How spread out the samples are. Smaller σ = more concentrated; larger σ = more dispersed. NekoServe fixes σ = 0.2μ to keep service times stable.
sigma.example: σ = 0.6 means about 68% of samples land within μ ± 0.6.

p.label: Success probability p
p.desc: The probability of drawing 1 in a Bernoulli trial. Always between 0 and 1.
p.example: p = 0.2 means on average 1 rest out of every 5 attempts.

cseat.label: Seat capacity c
cseat.desc: Number of servers in the M/M/c sense, but here seats act as passively-occupied servers. More seats = fewer customers get blocked out.
cseat.example: c = 10 means at most 10 customers can be seated simultaneously.

cstaff.label: Staff resource c
cstaff.desc: Number of servers in the second M/M/c — the staff pool that handles ordering and prep. One of the main bottlenecks in the shop.
cstaff.example: c = 2 is typical small-shop staffing: one takes orders, one preps.

arrInterval.label: arrivalInterval
arrInterval.desc: The 'average interarrival time' from the settings panel. Its reciprocal is λ and gets fed into the Exponential.
arrInterval.example: arrivalInterval = 8 min corresponds to λ = 1/8 customers/min.

Trun.label: Simulation horizon T_run
Trun.desc: How long the whole simulation runs. Longer runs give more stable stats, but execution time scales proportionally.
Trun.example: T_run = 240 is roughly one lunch shift — long enough to see the arc from open → peak → tail.

seatCount.label: Seats
seatCount.help: Total number of seats available. Fewer seats means longer queues.

rationale.meaning: Maximum number of customers seated at the same time.
rationale.why: Seats are the first constrained resource a customer hits after arriving. Excess customers queue and may abandon.
rationale.theory: Corresponds to the number of service stations c in an M/G/c multi-server queueing system.
rationale.defaultBasis: Small independent cafés typically have 6–15 seats. 10 is taken as the midpoint for a weekday scenario.
rationale.formulaHint: c is the number of servers in queueing notation. Here I treat seats as passively-occupied servers; default 10.

references.text: Hirsch, Navarro Rivero & Andersson (2025), Cats in a Cat Café, Animals 15(22):3233 — empirical cap of 14 customers
Kleinrock (1975), Queueing Systems Vol 1: Theory, Ch 3
Gross, Shortle, Thompson & Harris (2008), Fundamentals of Queueing Theory, 4/e

staffCount.label: Staff
staffCount.help: Staff taking orders and preparing food. Fewer staff means longer wait for food.

rationale.meaning: Number of staff who can simultaneously handle orders and preparation.
rationale.why: Staff is the second bottleneck after seating; it directly controls order- and meal-wait times.
rationale.theory: Second M/G/c queue. Ordering and preparation share one staff resource pool.
rationale.defaultBasis: Smallest common staffing in a small café: one front-of-house, one back-of-house.
rationale.formulaHint: Same M/M/c 'c', but for staff pool size. Ordering and prep share this pool; default 2.

references.text: Hirsch, Navarro Rivero & Andersson (2025), Cats in a Cat Café, Animals 15(22):3233 — observed staffing: 2 weekday / 3 weekend
Kleinrock (1975), Queueing Systems Vol 1: Theory, Ch 3
Gross et al. (2008), Fundamentals of Queueing Theory, 4/e

catCount.label: Cats
catCount.help: Number of cats available for interaction (excluding resting cats).

rationale.meaning: Total cats available for interaction with customers.
rationale.why: More cats means more visits, which extend dining times and reduce seat turnover.
rationale.theory: Each cat is an independent SimPy process (agent-based), not a passive queue resource.
rationale.defaultBasis: Commercial cat cafés typically have 3–5 visible cats. 3 is the scenario low-end.

references.text: Hirsch, Navarro Rivero & Andersson (2025), Cats in a Cat Café, Animals 15(22):3233 — 8–9 cats observed per site
Borshchev & Filippov (2004), From System Dynamics and Discrete Event to Practical Agent-Based Modeling
Macal & North (2010), Tutorial on Agent-Based Modelling and Simulation, J. Simulation 4(3)

customerArrivalInterval.label: Avg. customer arrival interval
customerArrivalInterval.help: Exponential distribution. Smaller value means more frequent arrivals (e.g. one every 5 min).

rationale.meaning: Average minutes between two consecutive customer arrivals.
rationale.why: Drives the entire simulation. All downstream metrics depend on it.
rationale.theory: Poisson process: interarrival times follow an exponential distribution and are memoryless.
rationale.defaultBasis: 8 min ≈ λ = 0.125 cust/min (~7.5/hour), between peak (λ≈0.2) and off-peak (λ≈0.05).
rationale.formulaHint: Read as: interarrival time T follows an Exponential distribution with rate λ = 1/8 (0.125 customers per minute). One customer every 8 minutes on average.

references.text: Dbeis & Al-Sahili (2024), Enhancing Queuing Theory Realism, J. Management Analytics 11(4):659–674 — empirical Poisson arrivals (22 cust/hr)
Hasugian, Vandrick & Dewi (2020), Queuing Models of Fast Food Restaurant, IOP Conf. Ser. MSE 851:012028 — alt. Weibull arrival empirics
Kleinrock (1975), Queueing Systems Vol 1: Theory, Ch 2
Ross (2019), Introduction to Probability Models, 12/e, Ch 5

maxWaitTime.label: Max wait tolerance
maxWaitTime.help: Maximum time a customer will wait for a seat before abandoning and leaving.

rationale.meaning: Longest time a customer will wait for a seat before abandoning.
rationale.why: Models customer abandonment so queues don't grow without bound, and provides the abandonment-rate metric.
rationale.theory: Corresponds to reneging / patience-time in queueing theory (M/G/c+G).
rationale.defaultBasis: Service-industry research places the abandonment threshold at 15–30 min. 25 is the scenario midpoint.

references.text: Ancker Jr. & Gafarian (1963), Some Queuing Problems with Balking and Reneging I, Operations Research 11(1):88–100 — classical reneging model (Exp patience)
Dbeis & Al-Sahili (2024), Enhancing Queuing Theory Realism, J. Management Analytics 11(4):659–674 — empirical reneging-rate analysis
Maister (1985), The Psychology of Waiting Lines, in: The Service Encounter, Lexington Books
Larson (1987), Perspectives on Queues: Social Justice and the Psychology of Queueing, Operations Research 35(6)
Haight (1959), Queueing with reneging, Metrika 2(1)

orderTime.label: Avg. order time
orderTime.help: Normal distribution, std = mean × 0.2, min 1 min. Time for staff to take an order.

rationale.meaning: Time for staff to help one customer finish ordering.
rationale.why: First segment of staff-side service time. Occupies the staff resource.
rationale.theory: Normal N(μ, σ²) with σ = 0.2μ, truncated via max(1, sample). Service times have a natural floor.
rationale.defaultBasis: Menu-and-checkout median is around 2–4 min. 3 min taken as midpoint.
rationale.formulaHint: Order time T follows a Normal distribution with mean 3 min and standard deviation 0.6 min (= 20% of the mean). max(1, sample) floors weird negatives.

references.text: Dbeis & Al-Sahili (2024), Enhancing Queuing Theory Realism, J. Management Analytics 11(4):659–674 — empirical service-time (Log-Normal, μ=2.01 min)
Law & Kelton (2015), Simulation Modeling and Analysis, 5/e, Ch 6 (input distribution selection)
Law (2010), Statistical Analysis of Simulation Output Data: The Practical State of the Art, Proc. 2010 Winter Simulation Conference, 65–74

preparationTime.label: Avg. preparation time
preparationTime.help: Normal distribution, std = mean × 0.2, min 1 min. Order-to-ready time.

rationale.meaning: Time from order confirmation to food served at the table.
rationale.why: Second segment of staff-side service time. Most visible wait from the customer's perspective.
rationale.defaultBasis: Hand-brewed or espresso-based drink with light meal: 6–10 min median. 8 taken as midpoint.
rationale.formulaHint: Prep time Normal, mean 8 min and standard deviation 1.6 min. The σ = 0.2μ rule is a service-time heuristic: stay concentrated, occasionally stretch.

references.text: Dbeis & Al-Sahili (2024), Enhancing Queuing Theory Realism, J. Management Analytics 11(4):659–674 — empirical service-time Log-Normal fit
Law & Kelton (2015), Simulation Modeling and Analysis, 5/e, Ch 6

diningTime.label: Avg. dining time
diningTime.help: Normal distribution, std = mean × 0.2, min 1 min. Time a customer spends eating.

rationale.meaning: How long a customer sits eating after receiving the order.
rationale.why: Directly controls seat occupancy time, i.e., seat turnover rate.
rationale.theory: Normal distribution, same structure as other service times.
rationale.defaultBasis: Scenario is "one drink + short stay" (not a full meal). Scale up manually (e.g., 45 min) for a lunch scenario.
rationale.formulaHint: Dining time Normal, mean 15 min and standard deviation 3 min. Drives seat turnover — longer dining means seats saturate faster.

references.text: Dbeis & Al-Sahili (2024), Enhancing Queuing Theory Realism, J. Management Analytics 11(4):659–674 — empirical service-time Log-Normal fit
Law & Kelton (2015), Simulation Modeling and Analysis, 5/e, Ch 6

catInteractionTime.label: Avg. visit duration
catInteractionTime.help: Normal distribution, std = mean × 0.2. Average minutes a cat spends at a customer's seat during one visit.

rationale.meaning: How long a cat lingers at one customer's seat during a single visit.
rationale.why: During a visit the customer cannot leave, so visit length extends dining time and lowers seat turnover.
rationale.theory: Normal(μ, σ²), σ = 0.2μ, same logic as service times.
rationale.defaultBasis: A typical friendly house cat stays a few to ten-plus minutes per visit. 10 min taken as midpoint.
rationale.formulaHint: Single cat visit duration Normal, mean 10 min and standard deviation 2 min. The customer is 'stuck' at their seat during this stretch.

references.text: Hirsch, Navarro Rivero & Andersson (2025), Cats in a Cat Café, Animals 15(22):3233 — cat-human contact interactions = 23.2% of observed time
Law & Kelton (2015), Simulation Modeling and Analysis, 5/e, Ch 6

catIdleInterval.label: Cat idle interval
catIdleInterval.help: Exponential mean. Average minutes a cat wanders before picking its next customer. Shorter means friendlier cats.

rationale.meaning: Average time a cat wanders after one visit before initiating the next.
rationale.why: Controls how often cats interrupt customers. The main knob for "cat friendliness".
rationale.theory: Exponential distribution, same family as customer arrivals (Poisson-style events).
rationale.defaultBasis: Scenario is "curious, friendly café cat", so 4 min. Try 1 min (very clingy) or 20 min (aloof) for teaching contrast.
rationale.formulaHint: Idle time between visits follows Exponential with rate 1/4 (mean 4 min). Smaller value = clingier cat.

references.text: Hirsch, Navarro Rivero & Andersson (2025), Cats in a Cat Café, Animals 15(22):3233 — cat-cat interaction rate 0.58/cat/hr; cat-human no-interaction 44.4%
Kleinrock (1975), Queueing Systems Vol 1: Theory, Ch 2
Ross (2019), Introduction to Probability Models, 12/e, Ch 5

catRestProbability.label: Rest trigger probability
catRestProbability.help: Probability (0–1) that a cat enters rest after finishing a visit. Resting cats do not initiate new visits.

rationale.meaning: Probability that a cat enters rest immediately after finishing a visit.
rationale.why: Prevents cats from running forever. Approximates an "energy budget" and makes the pool of available cats time-varying.
rationale.theory: Bernoulli trial, sampled independently after each visit.
rationale.defaultBasis: 0.2 means roughly one rest every 5 visits. Scenario value, not empirical.
rationale.formulaHint: Bernoulli = single coin flip. After each visit, flip once: 20% chance the cat naps, 80% chance it finds the next customer.

references.text: Hirsch, Navarro Rivero & Andersson (2025), Cats in a Cat Café, Animals 15(22):3233 — resting = 31.7%, out-of-sight = 10.7% of observed time
Ross (2019), Introduction to Probability Models, 12/e, Ch 4 (discrete random variables)

catRestDuration.label: Avg. rest duration
catRestDuration.help: Normal distribution, std = mean × 0.2, min 1 min. Length of each cat rest.

rationale.meaning: Average length of one cat rest period.
rationale.why: During rest the cat is unavailable, reducing overall interaction frequency.
rationale.defaultBasis: House-cat short naps are typically 5–15 min. 10 min taken as midpoint.
rationale.formulaHint: Rest duration Normal, mean 10 min and standard deviation 2 min. Cats on nap leave the available pool entirely during this window.

references.text: Hirsch, Navarro Rivero & Andersson (2025), Cats in a Cat Café, Animals 15(22):3233 — overall cat resting share 31.7%
Law & Kelton (2015), Simulation Modeling and Analysis, 5/e, Ch 6

simulationDuration.label: Simulation duration
simulationDuration.help: Total business minutes to simulate. 240 min = 4 h. No new customers arrive after this point.

rationale.meaning: Total simulated business time in minutes (240 ≈ 4 h).
rationale.why: Sets the sample size. Too short and averages are warm-up-dominated; too long and runs become slow.
rationale.theory: DES rule of thumb: run length ≥ 10× longest service time to approach steady state.
rationale.defaultBasis: 240 min ≈ one shift (e.g., 11:00–15:00 lunch block), enough to see full peak and cool-down.
rationale.formulaHint: Total simulated business time. 240 min ≈ one lunch shift. Too short → unstable stats; too long → slow runs.

references.text: Law & Kelton (2015), Simulation Modeling and Analysis, 5/e, Ch 9 (Output Analysis for Terminating Simulations)
Banks, Carson, Nelson & Nicol (2010), Discrete-Event System Simulation, 5/e, Ch 11

randomSeed.label: Random seed
randomSeed.help: Fix the seed to reproduce identical simulation runs for classroom comparisons.

rationale.meaning: Initial seed for the pseudo-random number generator.
rationale.why: A fixed seed makes the run fully reproducible, useful for classroom comparison and debugging.
rationale.theory: PRNG determinism: identical seeds generate identical pseudo-random sequences.
rationale.defaultBasis: 42 is a cultural reference ("the answer to life, the universe and everything"), mathematically arbitrary.

references.text: Knuth (1997), The Art of Computer Programming Vol 2: Seminumerical Algorithms, 3/e, Ch 3
L'Ecuyer (1999), Good Parameters and Implementations for Combined Multiple Recursive Random Number Generators, Operations Research 47(1)

warmUpDuration.label: Warm-up period
warmUpDuration.help: The simulation runs normally during warm-up, but metrics are not counted. Use this to eliminate initialization bias from an empty system. Set to 0 to disable.

validation.lowArrival: Arrival interval exceeds simulation duration; very few customers will arrive
validation.shortDuration: Simulation duration is short (< 30 min); results may be unstable
validation.extremeResources: Very large resource counts; simulation may be slow

batch.description: Run the same config with different seeds, get mean and confidence intervals

sweep.description: Sweep one parameter across a range and see how metrics respond
